data.title: Wall Street inches lower ahead of Fed decision and Big Tech earnings
data.description:
Wall Street trades lower on Wednesday as traders look ahead to big tech earnings and the Federal Reserve’s latest rate decision.

The blue chip Dow (DJI) was -0.6%, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.2%, and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.3%. 

From a sector-by-sector stance, nine of the 11 S&P segments were in the red, with materials at the bottom of the list. At the other end of the spectrum, energy was on top as oil (CL1:COM)  pushed back above $100 per barrel. 

U.S. Treasury yields topped 1-month highs. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 5 basis points to 4.40%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) ticked up 7 basis points to 3.91%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) added 4 basis points to 4.98%. 

The Federal Open Market Committee will decide on monetary policy, with rates expected to remain unchanged. The central bank decision is being made as Fed Chair Jerome Powell helms his last meeting, with Fed Chair nominee Kevin Warsh waiting in the wings. 

Elsewhere on the economic calendar, durable goods rebounded more than expected in March. Housing starts rose in March, while building permits fell. The U.S. international trade in goods deficit widened in March, and the wholesale and retail inventories accelerated M/M in March.

Big tech earnings data will continue to be in focus as Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN) will release their results after the bell. 

“Stocks are again trading at record highs, reflecting strong investor confidence, but the S&P 500's heavy concentration in the Mag 7 technology leaders elevates downside risk should earnings fall short, as valuations leave little margin for error. For now, current levels are supported by solid forward growth expectations, but that justification could quickly erode if margin compression emerges, inflation weighs on growth, or earnings signal a broader slowdown,” Chris Brigati, chief investment officer of SWBC, stated.

data.title: Roku Q1 2026 Earnings Preview: Bullish sentiment for the quarter and year ahead
data.description:
Roku (ROKU) is scheduled to announce Q1 earnings results on Thursday, April 30th, after market close.

The consensus EPS Estimate is $0.35 and the consensus revenue estimate is $1.2B (+17.6% Y/Y).

At least three Wall Street analysts praised Roku's (ROKU) Q4 print and highlighted multiple catalysts for growth at the company going into 2026.

Bank of America expects Roku to benefit in 2026 from various favorable industry trends along with certain company-specific actions, which include growth in CTV advertising, secular growth in streaming video domestically and internationally, and improving fill rates in the advertising business.

Morgan Stanley said that the size of Roku's user base and its ongoing execution on new monetization opportunities continue to position its platform as a key beneficiary of both healthy CTV ad spend and growing streaming services distribution revenues.

Evercore ISI called the report a "clean beat and raise" and expects tailwinds like the Amazon DSP integration, the home screen refresh, and U.S. political advertising to kick in over the balance of the year, with Roku Ad Manager to serve as "a multi-year growth driver."

SA contributor Shareholders Unite was bullish on the company, noting that international expansion, AI integration, and diversified ad monetization underpin ROKU’s strategy, with disciplined OpEx and significant buybacks supporting shareholder value.

An analysis by Julian Lin was also positive, highlighting “accelerating top-line growth and robust profitability, with management prioritizing sustained margin expansion and operational leverage.”

Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 5 upward revisions and 0 downward. Revenue estimates have seen 15 upward revisions and 0 downward.

data.title: Expand Energy outlines $0.20 margin uplift plan while targeting 7.5 Bcf/d at $2.85B capex
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Expand Energy (EXE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We generated $1.7 billion of free cash flow, inclusive of working capital inflows" and "used to reduce gross debt by $1.3 billion and returned over $290 million to our shareholders through base dividends and buybacks," said Chairman of the Board, Interim President & Interim CEO Michael Wichterich.
  • "Importantly, our full year production and capital guidance are unchanged," Interim CEO Wichterich said, while noting "our Gulf Coast assets were impacted" by Winter Storm Fern, "resulting in some shifting of CapEx from first quarter to second quarter."
  • "On our last call, we stated the size and the price of this effort is about $0.20 of margin improvement, which equates to approximately $500 million of repeatable incremental free cash flow per year," Interim CEO Wichterich said, adding, "we will be disciplined and create value by stacking singles and doubles" across premium markets, volatility, and new demand.
  • "Today, we announced a new offtake SPA with Delfin LNG for 1.15 million tons per year," Interim CEO Wichterich said. He added, "we see great value in this transaction as it's bigger, reaches market sooner and cheaper compared to our previous agreement, which has been terminated," and said, "our LNG strategy will be dynamic and shaped by the economic merits of each agreement, partnership or joint venture."
  • "We are kind of leading there within the industry. We're well below $3 now on a breakeven price," said Executive VP & CFO Marcel Teunissen, and "given the allocation of free cash in the first quarter... we can rebalance a little bit... to shareholder returns in the form of buyback."
  • "We continue to make progress on our operational efficiencies" and "we really haven't seen the impacts" from higher Haynesville rig activity "show up in our business yet," said Executive VP & COO Josh Viets.

Outlook

  • "Importantly, our full year production and capital guidance are unchanged," Interim CEO Wichterich said.
  • "We are in a position where we expect to deliver 7.5 Bcf a day at $2.85 billion of CapEx," COO Viets said, while also emphasizing flexibility: "if we see markets soften further, we'll absolutely be in a position to defer turn-in lines, slow down our completion activities."
  • "Q2 will end up being the high point of our CapEx for the year" and "as we get into the second half of the year, activity will moderate just slightly," COO Viets said, adding, "production will grow modestly across Q3 and Q4, again, assuming the market is there."
  • Management did not provide EPS or revenue guidance in this transcript, so a comparison to analysts’ estimates was not included.

Financial Results

  • "We generated $1.7 billion of free cash flow" and "returned over $290 million" via "base dividends and buybacks," Interim CEO Wichterich said.
  • "In the first quarter alone, we generated nearly $90 million of incremental value," Interim CEO Wichterich said, describing it as tied to the marketing/commercial focus on "monetizing volatility."
  • "We reduced most of the free cash flow we generated in the first quarter to reduce our gross debt" and "we can rebalance that allocation" toward buybacks after reaching the deleveraging goal, CFO Teunissen said.
  • The company did not disclose quarterly EPS or revenue figures in the spoken remarks provided.

Q&A

  • Matthew Portillo, TPH: Asked why Delfin was attractive and how LNG fits the portfolio; Chairman & Interim CEO Wichterich: "Delfin is the start... a foundational sort of contract" to "capture the LNG market opportunity and the premium pricing," and Executive VP Marketing & Commercial Daniel Turco: "this gives us all the integrated strategy" and "we're negotiating with them right now to be the gas supply manager."
  • Doug Leggate, Wolfe: Asked why CFO joined and how capital returns vs. deleveraging/hedging should work; CFO Teunissen: "risk management is a critical part of how we manage the business" and "think of our buyback program as opportunistic," adding, "we can walk and chew gum" and pointed to realized hedge prices "well above... the spot market."
  • Kevin MacCurdy, Pickering: Asked about well costs and service inflation, plus Western Haynesville results; COO Viets: "our costs have been stable... outside of some near-term inflation around diesel prices" and on Western Haynesville, "we've been very pleased with what we've seen to date" and "we will continue to work ourselves down the cost curve."
  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: Asked on CEO search timing and hedging philosophy; Chairman & Interim CEO Wichterich: "we're not waiting for a new CEO" and the search is "still kind of at the money" versus his timeline; CFO Teunissen: hedging the wedge "protect[s] the downside while we preserve the upside."
  • Zach Parham, JPMorgan: Asked if activity moderates at current strips and how remaining free cash flow is allocated; COO Viets: the plan is "predicated on that $3.50 to $4 price range" and "we'll always be responsive to pricing"; CFO Teunissen: after achieving the debt target, "we can now look at rebalancing that allocation... with share buybacks."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical on capital returns and activity, pressing on buybacks below breakeven and whether the strip drives slowing activity, including Leggate’s framing that gas is "below breakeven" and Parham asking if the company is "getting closer to a price" to moderate activity.
  • Management tone was slightly positive and execution-focused in prepared remarks, with confident framing such as "we have positioned ourselves to be in the right place at the right time" (Chairman & Interim CEO Wichterich) and repeated emphasis on acting despite the CEO transition: "we are not waiting for a new CEO to show up before we act" (Chairman & Interim CEO Wichterich).
  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on urgency around marketing and the move to Houston, this quarter kept the same core message but sounded more operationally concrete, citing specific Q1 cash flow, debt reduction, and the Delfin SPA.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • CEO transition messaging remained consistent: Q4 called for a CEO with a "bigger view of energy" and suggested "6" to "9 months" for the search; Q1 reiterated the search "remains on target" and that execution is ongoing: "the team is not waiting for a new CEO" (Chairman & Interim CEO Wichterich).
  • The $0.20 margin uplift goal persisted across both calls, with Q1 adding more near-term proof points, including "nearly $90 million of incremental value" from volatility and announcing the "1.15 million tons per year" Delfin SPA.
  • Capital allocation language shifted from Q4’s "balance sheet first" framing to Q1’s explicit rebalancing after achieving the debt reduction goal, with CFO Teunissen saying the company can "lean a bit more" toward buybacks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Weather and operational disruption risk was highlighted as "Winter Storm Fern" drove "some shifting of CapEx" into Q2, though guidance was maintained, according to Chairman & Interim CEO Wichterich.
  • Commodity price and strip risk was acknowledged through management’s flexibility statements, with COO Viets saying the company can "defer turn-in lines" and "slow down our completion activities" if markets soften.
  • Execution risk on downstream integration and LNG portfolio build was addressed through management’s emphasis on discipline and economics, with Chairman & Interim CEO Wichterich saying, "we do not believe that we have to swing for the fence" and CFO Teunissen framing buybacks as "opportunistic."

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Q1 cash generation funded rapid debt reduction and shareholder returns, while keeping full-year production and capital guidance unchanged despite storm-related timing impacts. The call focused on turning the $0.20 per unit margin uplift ambition into nearer-term commercial gains, highlighted by Q1 volatility monetization and a new Delfin LNG offtake SPA, while reiterating flexible operations and hedging as key tools amid gas-price volatility and as the CEO search continues on the previously discussed timeline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cognizant dips after Q2 revenue outlook miss; unveils cost-cutting plan
data.description:
Shares of Cognizant Technology (CTSH) dipped about 1% on Wednesday after the second-quarter revenue outlook came in below estimates.

The company narrowed the range of its full year revenue forecast. 

Q1 Metrics

The consulting services provider saw its adjusted EPS grow about 13.8% year-over-year to $1.40, which beat estimates. Meanwhile, revenue climbed about 5.8% year-over-year, or 3.9% in constant currency, to $5.41B, which came in line with analysts' estimates.

"In a complex macroeconomic environment, we delivered first quarter revenue growth in the upper half of our guidance range, with sustained bookings momentum and Financial Services again leading performance. We signed seven large deals in the quarter and delivered over 70% large deal total contract value growth year-over-year," said Cognizant's CEO Ravi Kumar S. 

The company said that on a trailing-12-month basis, bookings increased 11% year-over-year to $29.6B, which represented a book-to-bill of about 1.4x. Bookings in the first quarter increased 21% year-over-year.

Cognizant noted that first-quarter bookings included seven large deals, which are deals with a total contract value of $100M or greater, of which one was a mega deal, which is a deal with a total contract value of $500M or greater.

Project Leap

Cognizant said that in the second quarter of 2026, it introduced Project Leap, a program designed to accelerate its transformation by funding investments in its integrated offerings, AI capabilities and partnerships, reshaping productivity through competitive offerings, and upskilling its workforce The company is also resizing its workforce.

In connection with Project Leap, the company expects to record costs of $230M to $320M, with substantially all the costs expected to be incurred in 2026. This consists of $200M to $270M of employee severance and other personnel related costs and $30M to $50M of other charges. 

Cognizant noted that this program is expected to generate in-year savings of about $200M to $300M in 2026. The company added that the expected savings, net of the investments, are enabling it to raise its 2026 adjusted operating margin guidance from an expansion of 10 to 30 basis points to an expansion of 20 to 40 basis points, in line with the company's long-term aspiration to expand margins.

The company is planning to cut about 4,000 jobs, or nearly 1% of its workforce, Livemint reported citing people familiar with the matter.

During the company’s earnings call on Wednesday, management said more than 20,000 freshers would join this year, suggesting reductions in mid-level roles, the report added.

Cognizant said in an email to Seeking Alpha that it has not announced any numbers and cannot react to media speculation. The company referred Seeking Alpha back to its press release and first quarter earnings call.

Cognizant said in its earnings release that total headcount as of March 31, was 357,600, an increase of 6,000 from Dec. 31, 2025, and 21,300 from March 31, 2025.

In 2023, Cognizant reportedly planned to lay off about 3,500 staff.

Outlook

Cognizant expects second quarter revenue to be between $5.45B and $5.52B (midpoint at $5.485B), growth of 3.8% to 5.3%, or 3.2% to 4.7% in constant currency. The consensus revenue estimate is $5.56B.

The company now expects full-year 2026 revenue to be between $22.11 and $22.64B (midpoint at $22.375B) compared to the prior forecast range of $22.14B to $22.66B (midpoint at $22.4B). The  consensus revenue estimate is $22.36B.

Full-year 2026 adjusted EPS is expected to be in the range of $5.63 to $5.77 (midpoint at $5.7), growth of 7% to 9%. The  consensus revenue estimate is $5.65.

The company expects the full-year 2026 adjusted operating margin to be about 16% to 16.2%, or 20 to 40 basis points of expansion.

Buyback and Dividend

The company bought back 6.3M shares for $427M during the first quarter under its share repurchase program. As of March 31, there was $1.5B remaining under the share repurchase authorization.

In April, the company declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.33 per share for shareholders of record on May 18. This dividend will be payable on May 27.

Update: The story was updated with a statement from Cognizant about job cuts.

data.title: Phillips 66 surges after unexpected Q1 profit on higher refining margins
data.description:
Phillips 66 (PSX) up 6.7% in Wednesday's trading, making the stock one of the day's strongest gainers in the oil and gas sector on the S&P 500, after reporting a surprise Q1 profit, thanks to ​higher refining margins that helped offset steep losses tied to ‌volatile commodity prices.

As a result of the sharp increase in commodity prices during Q1, Phillips 66 (PSX) said its financial results were affected by $839M in mark-to-market losses related to short derivative positions used as economic hedges to manage price risk on certain physical positions; however, the increase in current market value of the associated underlying physical inventory is not reflected in book value under last-in, first-out accounting.

Phillips 66 (PSX) said its ​refining segment reported Q1 adjusted earnings of $208M, swinging from a loss of $937M in the year-earlier quarter, its realized margin rose to $10.11/bbl in the quarter from $6.81/bbl in a year ago, and U.S. refinery margins as ​measured by the 3-2-1 crack spread jumped 73% Y/Y on average​.

The refiner’s crude capacity utilization in the quarter reached 95%, compared with 80% ​from a year ago.

"We are confident in our ability to navigate market volatility due to our integrated business and the strength of our balance sheet," Phillips 66 (PSX) Chairman and CEO Mark Lashier said.

data.title: Delek targets at least $220M EOP run rate and outlines $520M-$560M 2026 DKL EBITDA amid Iran-driven market disruption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Delek US Holdings, Inc. (DK) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "I'm extremely pleased with our strong execution in the first quarter" and the quarter reflected "disciplined and successful execution of Big Spring turnaround" plus "continued progress on increasing our free cash flow profile through restructuring of our intermediation agreement and continued success of EOP," according to President, CEO & Director Avigal Soreq.
  • CEO Soreq tied current market conditions to geopolitical disruption: "The events in Iran have created many ripple effects in the markets, resulting in around 10 million barrels of crude production and approximately 5 million barrels per day of refining capacity remaining offline," adding, "We believe the structural product shortage created in this event will continue to impact the market well after the conflict comes to an end."
  • On operations and capital intensity, CEO Soreq said, "Big Spring successfully completed its planned turnaround" and "refinery is running at full capacity," and added, "With no further planned turnaround, we have the highest spending quarter behind us."
  • On the enterprise optimization plan, CEO Soreq said, "We are once again raising our enterprise optimization plan target to at least $220 million on an annual run rate basis" and noted, "During the first quarter of 2026, we estimate approximately $60 million of EOP contribution to our P&L."
  • On DKL and sum-of-the-parts, CEO Soreq said "DKL today reaffirmed its 2026 EBITDA guidance of $520 million to $560 million" and added, "In 2026, on a pro forma basis, with a continued growth in third-party cash flow, we expect DKL third-party EBITDA to exceed 80%" as part of a push toward "our deconsolidation goal."
  • CFO Mark Hobbs reported, "For the first quarter, Delek had a net loss of $201 million or $3.34 per share," while "Adjusted net income was approximately $5 million or $0.08 per share and adjusted EBITDA was approximately $212 million."

Outlook

  • CFO Hobbs gave Q2 throughput guidance by refinery: "72,000 to 77,000 barrels per day for Tyler, 78,000 to 83,000 barrels per day at El Dorado, Big Spring will run 65,000 to 70,000 barrels per day, and lastly, Krotz Springs will run 78,000 to 83,000 barrels per day," with "implied system throughput" of "293,000 to 313,000 barrels per day."
  • CFO Hobbs guided Q2 costs and below-the-line items: operating expenses "between $215 million and $225 million," G&A "between $47 million and $52 million," D&A "between $105 million and $115 million," and net interest expense "between $80 million and $90 million."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call framing (Q4 2025), management shifted from a turnaround-in-progress posture ("planned turnaround is progressing well and remains on track," CEO Soreq previously said) to a post-turnaround posture in Q1 2026 ("refinery is running at full capacity," CEO Soreq said).

Financial results

  • CFO Hobbs said Q1 adjusted results included discrete RFS items: "Excluding SREs, adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EPS were approximately $129 million and a loss of $0.98 per share, respectively," and "This removes the impact of our RVO exemption recognition for the first quarter of $82 million."
  • Segment detail highlighted a weak quarter in Supply & Marketing: "Supply and Marketing was a loss of approximately $61 million in the quarter," including "wholesale marketing had a loss of $27.1 million" and "Asphalt contributed a loss of $12.1 million," CFO Hobbs said.
  • Logistics remained a profit center in management’s narrative: "In the Logistics segment, we delivered our best first quarter to date, generating approximately $132 million of adjusted EBITDA," which "includes an approximate $10 million negative impact from winter storm Fern," CFO Hobbs said.
  • Cash flow and spending were positioned around the turnaround year: "Cash flow provided by operations was $461 million" and "Investing activities was a use of $190 million," while Delek standalone capital spending was "$181 million" and DKL investment was "$50 million," CFO Hobbs said.

Q&A

  • Alexa Petrick, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: With Big Spring complete, how should investors think about capital allocation priorities and SRE cash inflow? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "We have a very clear, crisp capital allocation program" and "We see a lot of value in our share price and more to come."
  • Petrick, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: How to think about Q2 captures amid macro volatility? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "we've seen the Strait of Hormuz closed" and "we see steep backwardation"; Executive Vice President of Strategy, Business Development & Investor Relations Bhardwaj: "there will be winners and losers in terms of capture rates" and "we are very well positioned."
  • Manav Gupta, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: Is U.S. refining advantaged vs. global peers? Executive Vice President Bhardwaj: "U.S. refining will have an advantage" and "the biggest winners will be the guys who have access to barrels even within the U.S."
  • Gupta, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: Why are SREs more likely/important with rising RINs? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "Risking SREs... will lead to higher price at the pump"; Executive Vice President Bhardwaj: "at $1.50 a gallon blended RIN price, our 2026 RVO compliance is close to $750 million" and "we definitely expect SREs to continue."
  • Matthew Blair, Tudor, Pickering, Holt & Co. Securities, LLC, Research Division: Is Big Spring improving and did the turnaround extend into Q2? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "We are very happy about what we see"; Executive Vice President Bhardwaj: "we are just being a little bit more conservative" on guidance.
  • Jason Gabelman, TD Cowen, Research Division: Outlook for Group 3 vs. Gulf pricing? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "The way we see a group today is actually stronger" and cited "2 pipelines" affecting dynamics "starting second half of this year" and later "3, 4 years down the road."
  • Douglas George Blyth Leggate, Wolfe Research, LLC: What RIN price assumptions and what brings RIN values down? Executive Vice President Bhardwaj: "based upon our current estimates and $1.50 a gallon blended D4, D6, D3 RIN price" Delek shows "a $750 million RVO obligation in 2026," and said "2026 SREs are extremely important to manage 2027 RIN bank."
  • Joseph Laetsch, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: What’s driving repeated EOP target increases and what about further upside? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "We are always looking to how to make it better" and also said, "we are seeing around $600 million to $700 million on a mid-cycle environment of free cash flow."
  • Laetsch, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: Path to deconsolidation/value unlock? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "Deconsolidation is our ultimate goal" and "We see that the current value... needs to have a 7 handle on this unit," while outlining potential paths including "selling assets" and "DKL has the ability to buy on its unit from DK."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated pressure on macro capture and RFS/RIN assumptions, including Leggate’s "the RIN... isn't $1.50, it's $1.90" and timing/scale questions on SREs.
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive/firm in RFS exchanges, emphasizing policy framing such as CEO Soreq’s "disproportionate economic harm" and Bhardwaj’s "What exactly EPA will do... they will figure it out."
  • Versus last quarter, Q1 2026 sounded more opportunity-focused on macro disruption and post-turnaround upside, while Q4 2025 emphasized "transformational year" execution and balance-sheet actions.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • EOP target language moved higher: Q4 2025 CEO Soreq said "raising our enterprise optimization plan target to at least $200 million," while Q1 2026 he said "raising... to at least $220 million" with an updated quarterly contribution estimate.
  • Supply & Marketing swung from profit to loss as described by the CFO: Q4 2025 "supply and marketing contributed approximately $23 million" (excluding SREs), while Q1 2026 "Supply and Marketing was a loss of approximately $61 million."
  • DKL separation messaging stayed consistent (both quarters referenced pro forma third-party EBITDA exceeding 80%), while Q1 2026 added operational progress: Q4 2025 "planned turnaround is progressing well" versus Q1 2026 "successfully completed" and "running at full capacity."

Risks and concerns

  • Management highlighted macro volatility tied to geopolitical events: CEO Soreq cited "dislocation between physical and paper grades" and "steep backwardation" as market features affecting outcomes.
  • RFS/RIN exposure remained central in both analyst questions and management framing, with Bhardwaj stating a 2026 view at "$1.50 a gallon blended" RIN pricing and discussing a potential "significant 2027 deficit" in the RIN bank if SRE levels are insufficient.
  • Execution risk was acknowledged indirectly through conservatism around post-turnaround guidance: Bhardwaj said Big Spring guidance reflects being "a little bit more conservative."

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a transition quarter following the Big Spring turnaround, paired with a higher EOP run-rate target of at least $220 million and an emphasis on capturing a disrupted crude/product market environment. In guidance, the company laid out Q2 throughput ranges totaling 293,000–313,000 bpd implied systemwide and cost ranges including $215–$225 million in operating expenses. In Q&A, analysts concentrated on capture rates, Group 3 dynamics, and the sensitivity of earnings and cash flow to RIN prices and SRE timing, while management repeatedly anchored its RFS position on "disproportionate economic harm" and maintained that DKL’s $520–$560 million 2026 EBITDA outlook and the push toward >80% third-party EBITDA remain key steps toward deconsolidation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Garmin maintains 2026 revenue outlook of ~$7.9B while fitness grows 42% in Q1
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Garmin Ltd. (GRMN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Consolidated revenue increased 14% to $1.75 billion, which is a new first quarter record." (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble)
  • "Gross and operating margins expanded to 59.4% and 24.6%, respectively, resulting in record first quarter operating income of $432 million, up 30% year-over-year and pro forma EPS of $2.08, up 29% year-over-year." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "With this in mind and consistent with our typical practice, we are maintaining the guidance issued in February, and we'll provide updates as the year unfolds." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "The primary driver of our performance is higher unit volumes, resulting in meaningful market share gains." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "The year-over-year margin compression was primarily due to higher tariff costs." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "We continue to achieve important milestones leading up to the launch of our next large-scale program with Mercedes-Benz, which we anticipate will drive significant growth starting in 2027 and beyond." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "We posted revenue of $1.753 billion for the first quarter, representing a 14% increase year-over-year." (CFO & Treasurer Douglas Boessen)

Outlook

  • "While the initial trends are encouraging, much of the year remains ahead." (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble)
  • "Looking forward, we expect second quarter outdoor performance to be similar to that of Q1." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "We also expect to achieve stronger performance in the back half of the year due to the timing of product launches, resulting in improved full year growth when compared to 2025." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "We are very pleased with the performance of aviation during the first quarter, and we expect to achieve solid growth throughout the remainder of the year." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "As a reminder, we expect auto OEM revenue to decrease in 2026 as the BMW program has reached peak volumes and as certain legacy programs approach end of life." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)

Financial Results

  • "Gross margin was 59.4%, a 180 basis point increase from the prior year quarter." (CFO & Treasurer Douglas Boessen)
  • "Operating expense as a percentage of sales was 34.8%, 110 basis point decrease." (CFO & Treasurer Boessen)
  • "We ended the quarter with cash and marketable securities of approximately $4.3 billion." (CFO & Treasurer Boessen)
  • "Inventory increased year-over-year and sequentially to approximately $1.9 billion." (CFO & Treasurer Boessen)
  • "During the first quarter of 2026, we generated free cash flow of $469 million" and "capital expenditures for the first quarter of 2026 were $67 million." (CFO & Treasurer Boessen)
  • "During the first quarter of 2026, we paid dividends of approximately $174 million, purchased $40 million of company stock." (CFO & Treasurer Boessen)

Q&A

  • Joseph Cardoso, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: "can you just touch on what is reinforcing" demand momentum and component-cost navigation? President, CEO & Director Pemble: "Registration rates are continuing to be strong" and "we are not experiencing" component cost pressure in current results, adding "we do have significant safety stock" and "I would expect that 2026 is still going to be somewhat muted, and we'll start to see some effect in 2027."
  • Joseph Cardoso, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked about private wearable challengers and subscriptions; President, CEO & Director Pemble said "customers want choices" and "we have been expanding our role in subscription-based services" including "Garmin Connect Plus."
  • Alyssa Shreves, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked about low-tier wearables; President, CEO & Director Pemble said "we're definitely seeing strong demand, both at the low end and the high end" within advanced wearables.
  • David S. MacGregor, Longbow Research LLC: asked whether Q1 was a heavier new-product quarter and the mix impact; President, CEO & Director Pemble said "we typically release somewhere around 100 new products a year" and "our revenue mix from new products tends to be pretty similar from year-to-year."
  • Benjamin Bollin, Cleveland Research Company LLC: asked about aviation OEM deliveries vs retrofit and backlog; President, CEO & Director Pemble said "new deliveries are definitely a strong contributor" and "Aviation aircraft makers are sitting on high backlog still."
  • Noah Zatzkin, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: asked about tariff policy changes and refunds; CFO & Treasurer Boessen said "we do expect there to be some tariff impact for the remainder of the year" and "we have not recorded any receivable or benefit for those refunds at this point in time."
  • Noah Zatzkin, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: asked marine trends and share; President, CEO & Director Pemble said "we saw particular strength on deliveries to builders" and cited "a very, very positive reaction to our new products, particularly the Spy pole and the 360 sonar."

Sentiment Analysis

  • "Hoping to get your thoughts" (Noah Zatzkin, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division) and "I was kind of taken aback" (Erik Woodring, Morgan Stanley, Research Division) reflected a slightly negative-to-pressing analyst tone focused on tariffs, input costs, and macro sensitivity.
  • "We're off to a great start in 2026" and "we are maintaining the guidance issued in February" (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble) reflected a slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, while Q&A responses emphasized caution with "much of the year remains ahead" and specificity on cost timing with "we'll start to see some effect in 2027." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • Compared with last quarter’s framing of supply chain risks, the current call used more near-term stability language on demand ("Registration rates are continuing to be strong") while keeping a similar caution on costs and uncertainty. (President, CEO & Director Pemble)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1, management reiterated guidance discipline without repeating the full-year numbers: "we are maintaining the guidance issued in February." (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble)
  • In Q4, management provided explicit 2026 targets: "We expect revenue to increase approximately 9% to $7.9 billion" and "This results in expected pro forma earnings per share approximately $9.35." (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble; CFO & Treasurer Douglas Boessen)
  • The segment narrative shifted from Q4’s expectation that "full year growth in Outdoor" would "accelerate in 2026" (President, CEO & Director Pemble) to Q1’s reported Outdoor decline and near-term pacing: "Revenue decreased 5%" and "we expect second quarter outdoor performance to be similar to that of Q1." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • Tariffs moved from Q4’s emphasis on mitigation and scenario planning to Q1’s financial statement impacts and accounting posture: "The year-over-year margin compression was primarily due to higher tariff costs" and "we do not record any benefit or receivable" for refunds. (President, CEO & Director Pemble; CFO & Treasurer Boessen)

Risks and Concerns

  • "We continue to outperform expectations in a business environment characterized by economic whiplash and geopolitical uncertainty." (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble)
  • "The year-over-year margin compression was primarily due to higher tariff costs" and management said refunds remain unbooked: "we have not recorded any receivable or benefit for those refunds at this point in time." (President, CEO & Director Pemble; CFO & Treasurer Douglas Boessen)
  • On input costs, management highlighted timing risk: "component costs come through our inventory" and "we'll start to see some effect in 2027," while saying 2026 is "well controlled" via "significant safety stock." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • On end-market sensitivity, management flagged potential hesitancy: "some of those markets like marine and aviation" can see "a little more hesitancy" tied to "oil prices and conflict" and added, for Marine, "customers start to express some worry." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)

Final Takeaway

Garmin’s management described Q1 as a record start driven by wearables-led Fitness growth and expanding consolidated margins, while keeping full-year guidance unchanged and emphasizing that Q1 is seasonally the lowest quarter. In Q&A, executives reinforced steady demand signals via registrations, positioned 2026 component-cost exposure as muted due to safety stock, and framed higher input costs as more visible in 2027. Key watch items remained tariffs (including unrecognized potential refunds), Outdoor’s near-term softness with a back-half launch-led recovery plan, and the Auto OEM transition toward a Mercedes-Benz ramp that management said should drive significant growth starting in 2027 and beyond.

data.title: Visa stock up with five blockchains added to stablecoin settlement pilot; CRCL, BLSH, GEMI down
data.description:
Visa (V) is adding five more blockchains to its global stablecoin settlement pilot, expanding its multi-chain settlement capabilities to nine blockchains.

The five new blockchains to be added are Arc, Base, Canton, Polygon, and Tempo.

The program has reached a $7B annualized stablecoin settlement run rate, up 50% since last quarter.

"Expanding our stablecoin settlement pilot program to more blockchains means our partners can choose the networks that best fit their needs, while relying on Visa to provide a common settlement layer across all of them," said Rubail Birwadker, Visa's global head of growth products and strategic partnerships.

Visa (V) was trading 9.21% higher at $337.78 during afternoon trading on Wednesday, Circle Internet Group (CRCL) was 2.12% down to $92.35, Bullish (BLSH) was 8.21% down to $36.55, and Gemini Space Station (GEMI) was 6.04% down to $4.20.

data.title: Molina wins double upgrade at BofA on Medicaid tailwinds
data.description:
Bank of America upgraded Molina Healthcare (MOH) to Buy from Underperform and lifted its price target to $250 from $152 per share on Wednesday, citing a favorable margin outlook for the company’s Medicaid business.

The analyst Kevin Fischbeck argued that Molina’s (MOH) Medicaid margins are likely to bottom out in 2026, with its path to reach target margins “being more a matter of time and math” as states slowly respond to trend/risk pool shifts in 2024/25, raising rates and margins next year.

“The dramatic changes in Medicaid enrollment since 2023 have created negative risk pool shifts, making it difficult for states to forecast costs and trend, resulting in margin pressure for the industry,” the analyst wrote.

Assuming the company’s long-term targets and normalized margins in 2029, Fischbeck expects Molina’s (MOH) earnings per share to improve by more than $30, generating significant upside for the stock over the next three years.

While there remains a large disparity in Medicaid rates/trends and uncertainty in the company’s other business areas, “MOH could conservatively establish a $30 2029 EPS target (vs. $5 in 2026), which, according to Fischbeck, is nearly twice as large as the consensus of $17.32.

data.title: Cognizant targets 16%-16.2% adjusted operating margin in 2026 through Project Leap
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cognizant (CTSH) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We delivered a solid first quarter with revenue growth in the upper half of our guidance range, expanded adjusted operating margin and strong bookings growth" (CEO & Director Ravi Kumar S).
  • "Q1 bookings grew 21% year-over-year" and "we signed 7 large deals with TCV of $100 million or greater, including 1 mega deal valued at more than $500 million" (CEO & Director Ravi Kumar S).
  • "We just announced a definitive agreement to acquire Astreya" and "upon closing, we believe Astreya will add a critical layer to our AI Builder technology stack" (CEO & Director Ravi Kumar S).
  • "Market conditions have become more complex since the start of the year, and we expect the impact from heightened macroeconomic uncertainty to persist in the near term" (CEO & Director Ravi Kumar S).
  • "In Q1, revenues of $5.4 billion, grew 3.9% year-over-year in constant currency" (Chief Financial Officer Jatin Dalal).
  • "First quarter adjusted operating margin of 15.6%" and "first quarter adjusted EPS was $1.40" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter, we expect revenue to grow 3.2% to 4.7% year-over-year in constant currency" and "this includes approximately 150 basis points from our recently completed acquisitions, including a partial quarter contribution from Astreya" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "Our full year revenue guidance is unchanged at 4% to 6.5% in constant currency" and "we expect large deal ramps and 2 full quarters of Astreya contribution to be meaningful second half drivers" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "The expected savings generated from the program, net of investments are enabling us to raise our 2026 adjusted operating margin guidance range to 16% to 16.2%" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "This leads to EPS guidance of $5.63 to $5.77" and "for 2026, we still expect to return approximately $1.6 billion of capital to shareholders" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).

Financial results

  • "On a trailing 12-month basis, bookings grew 11% and represented a book-to-bill of 1.4" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "Q1 gross margin decreased by 80 basis points year-over-year" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "First quarter free cash flow was approximately $200 million" and "during the quarter, we returned about $600 million of capital to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "We ended the quarter with cash and short-term investments of $1.5 billion or net cash of $949 million" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).

Q&A

  • Jason Kupferberg, Wells Fargo: asked about bookings mix and the full-year guide assuming improved discretionary spend in 2H; Chief Financial Officer Dalal said, "we don't exactly break out new versus renewal," and added, "the midpoint does assume a little better discretionary environment than what we assume for quarter 2."
  • Jason Kupferberg, Wells Fargo: asked whether AI is driving "irrational" pricing; CEO & Director Kumar said, "we have infused AI into our rate cards now" and "we are able to share the productivity and also keep it for ourselves."
  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: asked how token metering affects margins and whether token costs are billed to clients; CEO & Director Kumar said, "token metering is a reality" and described tokenized rate cards including "A3 is autonomous digital labor."
  • James Faucette, Morgan Stanley: asked about acquisition valuations and capital allocation; Chief Financial Officer Dalal said, "we have committed $1.6 billion to be returned to the shareholders" and discussed funding, "we have now used about $600 million" related to Astreya.
  • Tien-Tsin Huang, JPMorgan: asked whether Project Leap is offensive or defensive; CEO & Director Kumar said, "Leap is to make sure that we get there fast" and framed it as "our opportunity to resize our pyramid."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative in probing near-term visibility and pricing dynamics, with questions tied to "softening" discretionary spend and whether competition is becoming "irrational" (Kupferberg, Wells Fargo).
  • Management was slightly positive and repeatedly emphasized control and momentum, including "we remain confident" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal) and "we feel very confident" (CEO & Director Kumar), while acknowledging "heightened macroeconomic uncertainty" (CEO & Director Kumar).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s more celebratory tone around full-year outperformance, the current call used more cautionary macro language while still leaning on bookings and second-half ramp confidence.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Bookings language intensified quarter-over-quarter, shifting from Q4’s "Q4 bookings grew 9%" to Q1’s "Q1 bookings grew 21%" (CEO & Director Kumar).
  • M&A moved from closed/integration messaging around 3Cloud in Q4 to a new transaction, with Q1 announcing the Astreya agreement and positioning it as "a specialist in AI infrastructure build-out" (CEO & Director Kumar).
  • Margin framing changed from Q4’s reported "adjusted operating margin of 16%" to Q1’s "adjusted operating margin of 15.6%" alongside a new restructuring-style program, with Project Leap expected to drive "approximately $200 million to $300 million" of 2026 savings (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).

Risks and concerns

  • "We achieved these results against a softening demand environment" and "clients are appropriately cautious about making large investments" (CEO & Director Kumar).
  • "Policy changes are creating regulatory uncertainty in key areas of Health Sciences" and "trade policy uncertainty and supply chain disruptions remain realities" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • Project Leap execution risk and cost were outlined as "costs of $230 million to $320 million" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal), with mitigation centered on reinvestment and operating model changes.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as a combination of stronger large-deal booking momentum, a more cautious near-term macro backdrop, and a second-half setup tied to large-deal ramps and Astreya contribution, while introducing Project Leap as a cost-savings-and-reinvestment program that management said supports higher 2026 adjusted operating margin guidance and continued capital returns.
